title: The Sri Lankan political establishment’s ‘dirty tricks’ department seems to be in full swing as the nation is presented with a dizzying array of constitutional amendments under various titles
body_lines: The Sri Lankan political establishment’s ‘dirty tricks’ department seems to be in full swing as the nation is presented with a dizzying array of constitutional amendments, variously titled ’21st Amendment’, ‘21A’, ’21B’, ‘22A’ and so forth.

It says much for the combined idiocy underlying this process of ‘constitutional reform,’ (for want of a better term), that even the originators of these amendments are uncertain as to which clause belongs to what. Their bewildered faces and incoherent explanations when microphones are brandished before them, illustrates this very well.

Other politicians sitting in the opposition benches meanwhile enter into violent quarrels over the correct numbering of the amendments. All in all, this is constitutional Babel, in the Biblical sense of that term.

That is, ‘a confusion of tongues’ with no one meaning what they say and very few knowing what they mean. Clearly, there are two strands of disparate opinion which have emerged among the ‘faithful.’ The first urges the conditional acceptance of the Ministry of Justice’s constitutional amendment, one hesitates to give this a number in good conscience.

This looks at resuscitating the yahapalanaya’ inspired 19th Amendment with some devious twists. It disregards even the minimum balance of the 19th Amendment with the President being enabled to hold ministerial portfolios.

This is even as President Gotabhaya Rajapaksa returns to public prominence as he recovers from the unsettling fury of the youth’s ‘Aragalaya’ (struggle) and consolidates himself. Increasing number of state institutions are brought under his control while he lectures secretaries of ministries on productively using state land to grow crops to prevent a food crisis.

This is rich in manifold respects. The very reason why Sri Lankans are facing the spectre of starvation is due to intemperate Presidential policy making, not only in forcing a ridiculous overnight ban on commercial fertiliser but also refusing to reverse that policy.

Desperate farmers begged for relief. Instead, they were told that the Army would be used to ‘compel’ them to farm. But let us put that aside for the time being. The Government’s constitutional reform proposals are being touted by a Minister of Justice, the same worthy who, during the ‘yahapalanaya’ period, boasted that he would never allow the incumbent President, (at the time, the former Defence Secretary), to be arrested. The magnanimity given to the President in the holding of ministerial portfolios has been strongly objected to by the Opposition.

We await with bated breath, the next act in this drama as well as the fate of the Bill proposed by the main Opposition Samagi Jana Balawegaya which went much farther in correcting the constitutional imbalance of power than the Government’s draft.

We may also beware of purported ‘concessions’ that never really concede power from the political establishment to the furious citizenry. Professional lobbies and advocacy groups have called for the composition of the Constitutional Council (CC) to be changed so that politicians or their nominees will not hold the balance of power. This was a major problem with the 19th Amendment. That is essential rectification, the 17th Amendment is a good example to follow.

But even granted all of this, there is deep and legitimate suspicion that these are patchwork attempts to deflect public anger, to turn the focus towards constitutional reform which has never actually impacted on the populace.

And so we come to the second strand of opinion which ridicules the very idea of a President and a Parliament that allowed the nation to come to bankruptcy, its citizens to be humiliated and beggared before the world, being tasked with constitutional reform. That ridicule emphasizes that  this undermines the ‘Aragalaya’s call for ‘system reforms’ and for ‘Gota to Go.’

So, whence the Republic as its Fiftieth Anniversary arrives and departs without anyone the wiser? To answer that question, it must be recognised that the ‘Republic’ has never been a ‘Republic’ in the true meaning of that term. This is not only in the Rajapaksa age as some would like to believe.

Before the advent of the Rajapaksas, the ‘denial’ of the Republic was just better papered over by the gloss of ‘apparent’ conformity to the Rule of Law even when the same constitutional games were being played. The difference was that those games were far more sophisticated than the rough and tumble with crooks, charlatans and conners now.

I say this with force for there is a larger thrust to this argument. Even when the best judgments were being handed down by the Supreme Court (think, the mid to late eighties) upholding constitutional rights, from the right not to be arbitrarily arrested to freedoms of speech and expression, those were victories limited to constitutional theory scarcely percolating to the street. As public interest lawyers then, (not to be confused with the ugly commercialisation of public interest litigation later), it was thrilling to see constitutional rights advanced.

Even so, much depended on which President was in power at the time, even the (so-called) best legal brains thought strategy in that light. Little wonder then, that the ‘independence of the judiciary’ received short shrift as the apex court of the land and the Office of the Chief Justice became the target of unprecedented controversy during the Kumaratunga Presidency and after. So when the state of the Republic is reflected upon, awkward questions must be asked. How and why not only the Bar but also legal academia (largely) silent when the Bench embroiled itself in the thickets of political controversy?

Or when contempt of court was openly threatened against dissenters and when the judicial institution was taken in a determinedly political direction, was a Nelsonian eye turned? Those dagger thrusts were aimed at independent institutions while ‘many slept.’

In later decades, the very many ‘impeachments’ of Chief Justices and the loss of lustre of the judicial institution were all illustrative thereof. From that great sin comes all lesser sins including the crucifixion of Sri Lankan constitutionalism on the altar of political expediency, the monstrous growth of the Executive Presidency, the warping of the Offices of the Attorney General and the Inspector-General of Police (IGP).

The IGP has gone on record stating that he cannot control his own officers due to political appointments to key posts. That pathetic state cannot be simply rectified by ‘restoring’ an ‘independent’ National Police Commission or the ‘independent’ appointment of the IGP. Those inclined to argue the point may be encouraged to recall the ‘yahapalanaya’ example of an eminently unsuitable IGP who later went to jail over failures of command responsibility regarding the 2019 jihadist attacks on churches and hotels. All this is precisely why the 19th Amendment to the Constitution was doomed at the very point that it was born.

Needless to say, this will be the fate of yet another amendment which reinvents the wheel. The same Prime Minister in whose term, the 19th Amendment was birthed, has returned to that same seat to the hurrahs of some, issuing warnings of ‘worse times’ yet to come.

His warnings have little impact on the youth of the ‘Aragayala’ who celebrated their fifty days of struggle with a tad more colour than the Republic, protesting at a circus of constitutional amendments as hospitals run short of medicine with some instances reported where relatives of critically ill patients have assaulted doctors.
There are no saviours’ for Sri Lanka, let it be said plainly.

si.title: විවිධ මාතෘකා යටතේ රට කරකැවෙන ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධන මාලාවක් ජාතියට ඉදිරිපත් කරද්දී ශ්‍රී ලංකා දේශපාලන සංස්ථාපිතයේ ‘කුණු කූරු’ දෙපාර්තමේන්තුව දැඩි ලෙස ක්‍රියාත්මක වන බව පෙනේ.
body_lines: ‘21 වැනි සංශෝධනය’, ‘21 ඒ’, ‘21 බී’, ‘22ඒ’ යනාදී වශයෙන් විවිධ මාතෘකා යටතේ කරකැවෙන ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධන රාශියක් ජාතියට ඉදිරිපත් කරද්දී, ශ්‍රී ලංකාවේ දේශපාලන සංස්ථාපිතයේ ‘කුණු කපටි’ දෙපාර්තමේන්තුව සක්‍රීයව ක්‍රියාත්මක වන බව පෙනේ. .

මෙම ‘ව්‍යවස්ථාමය ප්‍රතිසංස්කරණ’ ක්‍රියාවලියට යටින් පවතින ඒකාබද්ධ මුග්ධත්වය සඳහා එය බොහෝ දේ කියයි, (වඩා හොඳ පදයක් අවශ්‍ය නිසා), මෙම සංශෝධනවල ආරම්භකයින් පවා කුමන වගන්තියට අයත් දැයි අවිනිශ්චිත බව. මයික්‍රෆෝන ඔවුන් ඉදිරියේ හංවඩු ගසන විට ඔවුන්ගේ ව්‍යාකූල මුහුණු සහ නොගැලපෙන පැහැදිලි කිරීම් මෙය මනාව විදහා දක්වයි.
විපක්‍ෂයේ බංකුවල අසුන් ගන්නා අනෙකුත් දේශපාලකයන් මේ අතර සංශෝධනවල නිවැරදි අංක තැබීම සම්බන්ධයෙන් දරුණු ආරවුල් ඇතිකර ගනී. සමස්තයක් වශයෙන් ගත් කල, මෙය ව්‍යවස්ථාපිත බාබෙල්, එම යෙදුමේ බයිබලානුකුල අර්ථයෙන්.

එනම්, තමන් කියන දෙය කිසිවකුට අරුත් නොදෙන සහ ඒවායින් අදහස් කරන දේ දන්නේ ඉතා සුළු පිරිසක් නොමැති ‘භාෂා ව්‍යාකූලත්වයක්’ යන්නයි. පැහැදිලිවම, ‘විශ්වාසවන්තයින්’ අතර මතුවී ඇති අසමාන මතයන් දෙකක් තිබේ. පළමුවැන්න අධිකරණ අමාත්‍යාංශයේ ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධනය කොන්දේසි සහිත ලෙස පිළිගැනීමට ඉල්ලා සිටින අතර, යහපත් හෘද සාක්ෂියක් ඇතිව මෙය සංඛ්‍යාවක් දීමට යමෙක් පසුබට වේ.
මින් පෙනී යන්නේ යහපාලනයේ ආනුභාව ලත් 19 වැනි ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධනය යම් වංක විකෘති කිරීම් සමඟ යලි පණ ගැන්වීමයි. ජනාධිපතිවරයාට අමාත්‍ය ධුර දැරීමට හැකිවීමත් සමඟ 19 වැනි සංශෝධනයේ අවම ශේෂය පවා නොසලකා හැර තිබේ.

ඒ, තාරුණ්‍යයේ ‘අරගලයේ’ (අරගලයේ) නොසන්සුන්කාරී කෝපයෙන් මිදී ශක්තිමත් වෙමින් ජනාධිපති ගෝඨාභය රාජපක්ෂ යළිත් මහජන ප්‍රසිද්ධියට පත්වන මොහොතකය. ආහාර අර්බුදයක් ඇතිවීම වැලැක්වීම සඳහා රජයේ ඉඩම් ඵලදායි ලෙස භෝග වගා කිරීම සඳහා යොදා ගැනීම පිළිබඳව අමාත්‍යාංශ ලේකම්වරුන්ට දේශන පවත්වද්දී වැඩිවන රාජ්‍ය ආයතන ප්‍රමාණයක් ඔහුගේ පාලනයට නතු වේ.

මෙය විවිධ ආකාරවලින් පොහොසත් ය. ශ්‍රී ලාංකිකයන් සාගතයේ අවතාරයට මුහුණ දීමට හේතුව වන්නේ වානිජ පොහොර සඳහා එක රැයකින් හාස්‍යජනක තහනමක් පැනවීම පමණක් නොව, එම ප්‍රතිපත්තිය ආපසු හැරවීම ප්‍රතික්ෂේප කිරීමත් සමඟ අසීමිත ජනාධිපති ප්‍රතිපත්ති සම්පාදනය නිසාය.

බලාපොරොත්තු සුන් වූ ගොවීන් සහන ඉල්ලා සිටියහ. ඒ වෙනුවට, ඔවුන් ගොවිතැන් කිරීමට ‘බල කිරීමට’ හමුදාව යොදා ගන්නා බව ඔවුන්ට කීවේය. නමුත් අපි ඒක දැනට පැත්තකින් තියමු. ආණ්ඩුවේ ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථා ප්‍රතිසංස්කරණ යෝජනා හුවා දක්වන්නේ, 'යහපාලනය' සමයේ, වත්මන් ජනාධිපතිවරයා (එවකට හිටපු ආරක්ෂක ලේකම්වරයා) අත්අඩංගුවට ගැනීමට කිසිවිටෙකත් ඉඩ නොදෙන බවට පුරසාරම් දෙඩූ එම සුදුස්ස අධිකරණ ඇමතිවරයෙකු විසිනි. . අමාත්‍ය ධූර දැරීමේදී ජනාධිපතිවරයාට දක්වන උදාරත්වය විපක්ෂයේ දැඩි විරෝධයට ලක්ව තිබේ.

මේ නාටකයේ මීළඟ ක්‍රියාව මෙන්ම ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථාමය අසමතුලිතතාවය නිවැරදි කිරීමට ආණ්ඩුවේ කෙටුම්පතට වඩා බොහෝ දුර ගිය ප්‍රධාන විපක්ෂය වන සමගි ජන බලවේගය යෝජනා කළ පනත් කෙටුම්පතට අත්වන ඉරණම ගැන අපි හුස්ම හිරකරගෙන බලා සිටිමු.

දේශපාලන සංස්ථාපිතයේ සිට කෝපයට පත් පුරවැසියන්ට කිසි විටෙකත් බලය ලබා නොදෙන යැයි කියනු ලබන 'සහන' ගැන ද අපි පරෙස්සම් විය හැකිය. දේශපාලකයන්ට හෝ ඔවුන් විසින් නම් කරන ලද අයට බල තුලනය හිමි නොවන පරිදි ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථා සභාවේ (CC) සංයුතිය වෙනස් කරන ලෙස වෘත්තීය ලොබි සහ උපදේශක කණ්ඩායම් ඉල්ලා ඇත. 19 වැනි ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධනයේදී මෙය විශාල ගැටලුවක් විය. එය අත්‍යවශ්‍ය නිවැරදි කිරීමක්, 17 වැනි සංශෝධනය අනුගමනය කිරීමට හොඳ උදාහරණයක්.

එහෙත් මේ සියල්ල ලබා දී තිබියදීත්, මේවා මහජන කෝපය වෙනතකට යොමු කිරීමටත්, කිසි දිනෙක ජනතාවට සැබවින්ම බලපෑමක් නොකළ ව්‍යවස්ථාමය ප්‍රතිසංස්කරණ දෙසට අවධානය යොමු කිරීමටත් ගන්නා පැච් වර්ක් උත්සාහයන් බවට ගැඹුරු සහ නීත්‍යානුකූල සැකයක් පවතී.

ඒ නිසා අපි ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථා ප්‍රතිසංස්කරණය කිරීමේ කාර්යය භාර ගනිමින් ජාතිය බංකොලොත් වීමටත්, එහි පුරවැසියන් ලෝකය ඉදිරියේ අවමානයට හා හිඟාකෑමටත් ඉඩ දුන් ජනාධිපතිවරයකු සහ පාර්ලිමේන්තුවක් පිළිබඳ අදහස හාස්‍යයට පත් කරන දෙවන මතයට පැමිණෙමු. ‘පද්ධති ප්‍රතිසංස්කරණ’ සහ ‘ගෝඨාට යන්න’ යන ‘අරගලයාගේ හඬ’ මෙයින් හෑල්ලුවට ලක්වන බව එම උපහාසය අවධාරණය කරයි.

එසේ නම්, ජනරජය එහි පනස් වැනි සංවත්සරය ලෙස පැමිණෙන්නේ සහ ප්‍රඥාවන්ත කිසිවෙකු නොමැතිව පිටව යන්නේ කොතැනින්ද? එම ප්‍රශ්නයට පිළිතුරු දීමට නම්, එම යෙදුමේ සැබෑ අර්ථයෙන් ‘ජනරජය’ කිසිදා ‘ජනරජයක්’ වී නැති බව පිළිගත යුතුය. මෙය සමහරු විශ්වාස කිරීමට කැමති රාජපක්ෂ යුගයේ පමණක් නොවේ.

රාජපක්ෂලාගේ ආගමනයට පෙර, ජනරජයේ ‘ප්‍රතික්ෂේප කිරීම’, එම ව්‍යවස්ථාමය ක්‍රීඩා ක්‍රියාත්මක වන විට පවා නීතියේ ආධිපත්‍යයට ‘පෙනෙන’ අනුකූලතාවයේ ඔප දැමීමෙන් වඩාත් හොඳින් කඩදාසි කර තිබුණි. වෙනස වූයේ එම ක්‍රීඩා දැන් වංචනිකයින්, චාලටන්වරුන් සහ කෝනර්ස් සමඟ ඇති රළු හා පැටලීමට වඩා ඉතා සංකීර්ණ වීමයි.

මම මෙය බලහත්කාරයෙන් කියන්නේ මෙම තර්කයට විශාල තල්ලුවක් ඇති බැවිනි. අත්තනෝමතික ලෙස අත්අඩංගුවට නොගැනීමේ අයිතියේ සිට භාෂණයේ සහ ප්‍රකාශනයේ නිදහස දක්වා ව්‍යවස්ථාමය අයිතිවාසිකම් තහවුරු කරමින් ශ්‍රේෂ්ඨාධිකරණය (හිතන්න, අසූව දශකයේ මැද භාගයේ සිට) හොඳම තීන්දු දෙන විට පවා ඒවා ව්‍යවස්ථාපිත න්‍යායට පමණක් සීමා වූ ජයග්‍රහණ ය. වීදියට. මහජන අවශ්‍යතා නීතීඥයින් ලෙස, (පසුව මහජන අවශ්‍යතා නඩු කැත වාණිජකරණය සමඟ පටලවා නොගත යුතුය), ව්‍යවස්ථාපිත අයිතිවාසිකම් ඉදිරියට යෑම දැකීම සතුටට කරුණකි.

එසේ වුවද, බොහෝ දේ රඳා පවතින්නේ එවකට බලයේ සිටියේ කුමන ජනාධිපතිවරයාද යන්න මතය, (ඊනියා) හොඳම නීතිමය මොළය පවා එම ආලෝකයේ උපාය මාර්ග සිතුවේය. කුමාරතුංග ජනාධිපති ධූරය දැරූ සමයේ සහ ඉන් පසුවත් අගවිනිසුරු කාර්යාලය පෙර නොවූ විරූ ආන්දෝලනයකට තුඩු දුන් දේශයේ ශ්‍රේෂ්ඨාධිකරණය ලෙසින් ‘අධිකරණයේ ස්වාධීනත්වය’ කෙටි කෙටීමකට ලක්වීම පුදුමයක් නොවේ. එබැවින් ජනරජයේ රාජ්‍යය පිළිබිඹු කරන විට, අමුතු ප්‍රශ්න ඇසිය යුතුය. විනිසුරු මඩුල්ල දේශපාලන මතභේදයේ පැටලී සිටින විට නීතිඥ මණ්ඩලය පමණක් නොව නීති විද්‍යාඥයන් (විශාල වශයෙන්) නිහඬ වූයේ කෙසේද සහ ඇයි?

එසේත් නැතිනම් විරුද්ධ මත දරන්නන්ට එරෙහිව උසාවියට ​​අපහාස කිරීම විවෘතව තර්ජනය කරන විට සහ අධිකරණ ආයතනය අධිෂ්ඨානශීලී දේශපාලන දිශානතියකට ගෙන යන විට නෙල්සෝනියානු ඇස යොමු වූයේද? එම කිනිතුල්ලන් ස්වාධීන ආයතන වෙත එල්ල වූ අතර ‘බොහෝ දෙනෙක් නිදාගත්හ.

පසු දශක කිහිපය තුළ, අගවිනිසුරුවරුන්ගේ ‘දෝෂාභියෝග’ බොහෝමයක් සහ අධිකරණ ආයතනයේ දීප්තිය නැතිවීම යන සියල්ල එයින් නිදර්ශන විය. එම මහා පාපයෙන් ශ්‍රී ලංකා ව්‍යවස්ථාවාදය දේශපාලන අවශ්‍යතා පූජාසනය මත කුරුසියේ ඇණ ගැසීම, විධායක ජනාධිපති ක්‍රමයේ බිහිසුණු වර්ධනය, නීතිපති සහ පොලිස්පති (අයිජීපී) ගේ කාර්යාල දඟලනු ලැබීම ඇතුළු සියලු අඩු පාපයන් පැමිණේ.

දේශපාලන ප‍්‍රධාන තනතුරුවලට පත්කිරීම් හේතුවෙන් තම නිලධාරීන් පාලනය කර ගැනීමට නොහැකි බව පොලිස්පතිවරයා ප‍්‍රකාශ කර තිබේ. ‘ස්වාධීන’ ජාතික පොලිස් කොමිසමක් ‘ප්‍රතිෂ්ඨාපනය’ කිරීමෙන් හෝ පොලිස්පතිවරයාගේ ‘ස්වාධීන’ පත්කිරීමෙන් එම දුක්ඛිත තත්ත්වය සරලව නිවැරදි කළ නොහැකිය. 2019 පල්ලි සහ හෝටල්වලට එල්ල වූ ජිහාඩ් ප්‍රහාර සම්බන්ධයෙන් අණදීමේ වගකීම පැහැර හැරීම හේතුවෙන් පසුව සිරගත වූ විශිෂ්ට ලෙස නොගැලපෙන පොලිස්පතිවරයෙකුගේ 'යහපාලනය' උදාහරණය සිහිපත් කිරීමට කාරණය තර්ක කිරීමට කැමති අය දිරිමත් කළ හැකිය. මේ සියල්ල හරියටම 19 වන ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධනය බිහි වූ මොහොතේම විනාශයට පත් වූයේ මන්ද යන්නයි.

රෝදය ප්‍රතිනිර්මාණය කරන තවත් සංශෝධනයක ඉරණම මෙය වනු ඇති බව අමුතුවෙන් කිව යුතු නැත. 19 වැනි ආණ්ඩුක්‍රම ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධනය බිහිකළ අගමැතිතුමාම ඇතැමුන්ගේ හුරතලයට යළිත් එම ආසනයටම පැමිණ ඇත්තේ ‘නරක කාලය’ ගැන අනතුරු ඇඟවීම් ද නිකුත් කරමිනි.

රෝහල්වල බෙහෙත් හිඟවීම නිසා ව්‍යවස්ථා සංශෝධන සර්කස් එකකට විරෝධය පළ කරමින් ජනරජයට වඩා තරමක් වර්ණයෙන් දින පනහක අරගලය සැමරූ ‘අරගයාල’ තරුණ පරපුරට ඔහුගේ අනතුරු ඇඟවීම් බලපාන්නේ නැති තරම්ය. රෝගින් වෛද්‍යවරුන්ට පහර දී තිබෙනවා.
ශ්‍රී ලංකාවට ගැලවුම් කරුවන් නැත, එය පැහැදිලිව කිව යුතුය.

ta.title: இலங்கை அரசியல் ஸ்தாபனத்தின் ‘அழுக்காறு தந்திரங்கள்’ துறை முழு வீச்சில் பல்வேறு தலைப்புகளின் கீழ் தேசம் தலை சுற்றும் வகையில் அரசியலமைப்புத் திருத்தங்களை முன்வைத்து வருகிறது.
body_lines: '21வது திருத்தம்', '21ஏ', '21பி', '22ஏ' மற்றும் பலவிதமான தலைப்பிலான அரசியலமைப்புத் திருத்தங்களின் தலைச்சுற்றல் வரிசையுடன் தேசம் முன்வைக்கப்படுவதால், இலங்கை அரசியல் ஸ்தாபனத்தின் 'கெட்ட தந்திரங்கள்' துறை முழு வீச்சில் இருப்பதாகத் தெரிகிறது. .

இந்த ‘அரசியலமைப்புச் சீர்திருத்தம்’ (ஒரு சிறந்த சொல் தேவை) என்ற இந்தச் செயல்பாட்டின் அடிப்படையிலான ஒருங்கிணைந்த முட்டாள்தனத்தைப் பொறுத்தவரை, இந்தத் திருத்தங்களைத் தோற்றுவித்தவர்கள் கூட எந்த உட்பிரிவைச் சேர்ந்தது என்பது நிச்சயமற்றதாகவே உள்ளது. அவர்களின் குழப்பமான முகங்கள் மற்றும் ஒலிவாங்கிகள் அவர்களுக்கு முன்னால் முத்திரை குத்தப்படும் போது பொருத்தமற்ற விளக்கங்கள் இதை நன்றாக விளக்குகின்றன.

இதற்கிடையில் எதிர்க்கட்சி பெஞ்சில் அமர்ந்திருக்கும் மற்ற அரசியல்வாதிகள் திருத்தங்களின் சரியான எண்ணிக்கையில் வன்முறை சண்டையில் ஈடுபடுகின்றனர். மொத்தத்தில், இது அரசியலமைப்பு பாபெல், அந்த வார்த்தையின் பைபிள் அர்த்தத்தில்.

அதாவது, ‘நாக்குகளின் குழப்பம்’ அவர்கள் சொல்வதை யாரும் அர்த்தப்படுத்தாமல், அவர்கள் என்ன சொல்கிறார்கள் என்பதை மிகக் குறைவானவர்களே அறிவார்கள். தெளிவாக, 'விசுவாசிகளிடையே' இரு வேறுபட்ட கருத்துக்கள் தோன்றியுள்ளன. முதலாவது நீதி அமைச்சின் அரசியலமைப்பு திருத்தத்தை நிபந்தனையுடன் ஏற்றுக்கொள்ள வேண்டும் என்று வலியுறுத்துகிறது, ஒருவர் நல்ல மனசாட்சியுடன் இந்த எண்ணைக் கொடுக்கத் தயங்குகிறார்.
இது சில வஞ்சகமான திருப்பங்களுடன் யஹபாலனயாவால் ஈர்க்கப்பட்ட 19 வது திருத்தத்தை மீண்டும் உயிர்ப்பிப்பதைப் பார்க்கிறது. 19வது திருத்தச் சட்டத்தின் குறைந்தபட்ச இருப்புத் தொகையைக் கூட அது புறக்கணிக்கிறது.

ஜனாதிபதி கோட்டாபய ராஜபக்ஷ இளைஞர்களின் ‘அறகலய’ (போராட்டம்) என்ற அமைதியற்ற சீற்றத்தில் இருந்து மீண்டு தன்னை பலப்படுத்திக் கொள்ளும் போது, ​​பொது முக்கியத்துவத்திற்குத் திரும்பியபோதும் இது உள்ளது. உணவு நெருக்கடியைத் தடுப்பதற்காக பயிர்களை வளர்ப்பதற்கு அரச காணிகளை உற்பத்தி ரீதியில் பயன்படுத்துவது குறித்து அமைச்சுக்களின் செயலாளர்களுக்கு விரிவுரைகளை வழங்கும் போது அதிகரித்து வரும் அரச நிறுவனங்களின் எண்ணிக்கை அவரது கட்டுப்பாட்டின் கீழ் கொண்டுவரப்படுகிறது.

இது பன்மடங்கு அம்சங்களில் நிறைந்துள்ளது. இலங்கையர்கள் பட்டினிச் சாவை எதிர்கொள்வதற்குக் காரணம், வர்த்தக உரங்களுக்கு ஒரே இரவில் அபத்தமான தடையை விதித்ததோடு மட்டுமல்லாமல், அந்தக் கொள்கையைத் திரும்பப் பெற மறுத்தும், மிதமிஞ்சிய ஜனாதிபதியின் கொள்கை வகுக்கும் காரணமாகும்.

விரக்தியடைந்த விவசாயிகள் நிவாரணம் கோரி மன்றாடினர். மாறாக, அவர்களை விவசாயம் செய்ய 'நிர்பந்திக்க' ராணுவம் பயன்படுத்தப்படும் என்று கூறப்பட்டது. ஆனால் அதை இப்போதைக்கு ஒதுக்கி வைப்போம். அரசாங்கத்தின் அரசியலமைப்பு சீர்திருத்த முன்மொழிவுகள், நீதி அமைச்சர் ஒருவரால், 'யஹபாலனய' காலத்தில், தற்போதைய ஜனாதிபதியை (அந்த நேரத்தில், முன்னாள் பாதுகாப்புச் செயலாளரைக்) கைது செய்ய அனுமதிக்க மாட்டேன் என்று பெருமையடித்துக் கொண்டிருந்தார். . அமைச்சுப் பதவிகளை வகிப்பதில் ஜனாதிபதிக்கு வழங்கப்பட்ட பெருந்தன்மைக்கு எதிர்க்கட்சிகள் கடும் எதிர்ப்புத் தெரிவித்துள்ளன.

நாங்கள் மூச்சுத் திணறலுடன் காத்திருக்கிறோம், இந்த நாடகத்தின் அடுத்த செயல் மற்றும் முக்கிய எதிர்க்கட்சியான சமகி ஜன பலவேகயா முன்மொழியப்பட்ட மசோதாவின் தலைவிதி, இது அரசாங்கத்தின் வரைவை விட அதிகாரத்தின் அரசியலமைப்பு ஏற்றத்தாழ்வை சரிசெய்வதில் அதிக தூரம் சென்றது.

அரசியல் ஸ்தாபனத்திலிருந்து கோபமடைந்த குடிமக்களுக்கு அதிகாரத்தை ஒருபோதும் ஒப்புக்கொள்ளாத 'சலுகைகள்' குறித்தும் நாம் எச்சரிக்கையாக இருக்கலாம். அரசியல் நிர்ணய சபையின் (CC) அமைப்பு மாற்றப்பட வேண்டும் என்று தொழில்முறை லாபிகள் மற்றும் வக்கீல் குழுக்கள் அழைப்பு விடுத்துள்ளன, இதனால் அரசியல்வாதிகள் அல்லது அவர்களின் வேட்பாளர்கள் அதிகார சமநிலையை வைத்திருக்க மாட்டார்கள். இது 19வது திருத்தச் சட்டத்தில் பெரும் சிக்கலாக இருந்தது. இது இன்றியமையாத திருத்தம், 17வது திருத்தம் பின்பற்றுவதற்கு ஒரு சிறந்த உதாரணம்.
ஆனால் இவை அனைத்தும் வழங்கப்பட்டாலும் கூட, இவை பொதுமக்களின் கோபத்தைத் திசைதிருப்ப, அரசியலமைப்புச் சீர்திருத்தத்தின் மீது கவனம் செலுத்தும் முயற்சிகள் என்று ஆழமான மற்றும் நியாயமான சந்தேகம் உள்ளது.

எனவே, அரசியலமைப்புச் சீர்திருத்தத்திற்குப் பணிக்கப்பட்ட, தேசம் திவாலாவதற்கும், அதன் குடிமக்கள் அவமானப்படுத்தப்படுவதற்கும், பிச்சையெடுப்பதற்கும் இடமளித்த ஜனாதிபதி மற்றும் பாராளுமன்றத்தின் யோசனையை கேலி செய்யும் இரண்டாவது கருத்துக்கு நாங்கள் வருகிறோம். இது ‘அமைப்பு சீர்திருத்தங்கள்’ மற்றும் ‘கோதா செல்ல வேண்டும்’ என்ற ‘அரகலயாவின் அழைப்பை குறைமதிப்பிற்கு உட்படுத்துகிறது’ என்பதை அந்த கேலி வலியுறுத்துகிறது.

அப்படியென்றால், குடியரசு அதன் ஐம்பதாவது ஆண்டு நிறைவாக எங்கிருந்து வருகிறது மற்றும் அறிவாளிகள் யாரும் இல்லாமல் செல்கிறது? அந்தக் கேள்விக்கு பதிலளிக்க, அந்த வார்த்தையின் உண்மையான அர்த்தத்தில் 'குடியரசு' ஒருபோதும் 'குடியரசு' ஆக இருந்ததில்லை என்பதை அங்கீகரிக்க வேண்டும். சிலர் நம்ப விரும்புவது போல் இது ராஜபக்ச காலத்தில் மட்டும் இல்லை.

ராஜபக்ஷக்களின் வருகைக்கு முன்னர், அதே அரசியலமைப்பு விளையாட்டுகள் விளையாடப்பட்டபோதும் கூட, சட்டத்தின் ஆட்சிக்கு 'வெளிப்படையான' இணக்கத்தின் பளபளப்பினால் குடியரசின் 'மறுப்பு' சிறப்பாக எழுதப்பட்டது. வித்தியாசம் என்னவென்றால், அந்த விளையாட்டுகள் இப்போது க்ரூக்ஸ், சார்லட்டன்கள் மற்றும் கோனர்கள் கொண்ட கரடுமுரடான மற்றும் டம்பிள்களை விட மிகவும் அதிநவீனமாக இருந்தன.

இந்த வாதத்திற்கு ஒரு பெரிய உந்துதல் இருப்பதால் நான் இதை வலிமையுடன் சொல்கிறேன். உச்ச நீதிமன்றத்தால் (எண்பதுகளின் நடுப்பகுதியிலிருந்து பிற்பகுதி வரை) அரசியலமைப்பு உரிமைகளை நிலைநிறுத்தி, தன்னிச்சையாக கைது செய்யப்படாத உரிமை முதல் பேச்சு மற்றும் கருத்துச் சுதந்திரம் வரை சிறந்த தீர்ப்புகள் வழங்கப்பட்டபோதும், அவை அரசியலமைப்பு கோட்பாடுகளுக்கு மட்டுப்படுத்தப்பட்ட வெற்றிகள். தெருவுக்கு. பொதுநல வழக்குரைஞர்களாக, (பின்னர் பொதுநல வழக்குகளின் அசிங்கமான வணிகமயமாக்கலுடன் குழப்பமடையக்கூடாது), அரசியலமைப்பு உரிமைகள் முன்னேறுவதைப் பார்ப்பது சிலிர்ப்பாக இருந்தது.

அப்படியிருந்தும், அந்த நேரத்தில் எந்த ஜனாதிபதி ஆட்சியில் இருந்தார் என்பதைப் பொறுத்தது, (என்று அழைக்கப்படும்) சிறந்த சட்ட மூளைகள் கூட அந்த வெளிச்சத்தில் உத்தியை நினைத்தன. குமாரதுங்க ஜனாதிபதியாக இருந்த காலத்திலும் அதற்குப் பின்னரும் வரலாறு காணாத சர்ச்சைக்கு இலக்காகி பிரதம நீதியரசரின் அலுவலகம் மற்றும் பிரதம நீதியரசர் அலுவலகம் நாட்டின் உச்ச நீதிமன்றமாக 'நீதித்துறையின் சுதந்திரம்' குறுகிய கால இடைவெளியைப் பெற்றதில் ஆச்சரியமில்லை. எனவே குடியரசின் நிலையைப் பிரதிபலிக்கும் போது, ​​மோசமான கேள்விகள் கேட்கப்பட வேண்டும். பெஞ்ச் அரசியல் சர்ச்சையில் சிக்கியபோது பார் மட்டுமல்ல, சட்டக் கல்வியாளர்களும் (பெரும்பாலும்) அமைதியாக இருப்பது எப்படி, ஏன்?

அல்லது எதிர்ப்பாளர்களுக்கு எதிராக நீதிமன்ற அவமதிப்பு வெளிப்படையாக அச்சுறுத்தப்பட்டபோது மற்றும் நீதித்துறை நிறுவனம் உறுதியான அரசியல் திசையில் கொண்டு செல்லப்பட்டபோது, ​​நெல்சோனியனின் கண் திரும்பியதா? ‘பலர் உறங்கும்போது’ அந்த குத்துச்சண்டைகள் சுயாதீன நிறுவனங்களை குறிவைத்தன.

பிந்தைய தசாப்தங்களில், தலைமை நீதிபதிகளின் பல 'இம்பீச்மென்ட்கள்' மற்றும் நீதித்துறை நிறுவனத்தின் பொலிவை இழந்தது அனைத்தும் அதற்கு விளக்கமாக இருந்தன. அந்த மாபெரும் பாவத்தில் இருந்து, இலங்கை அரசியலமைப்பு அரசியல் பலிபீடத்தின் மீது சிலுவையில் அறையப்பட்டது, நிறைவேற்று அதிகாரம் கொண்ட ஜனாதிபதி பதவியின் கொடூரமான வளர்ச்சி, சட்டமா அதிபர் மற்றும் பொலிஸ் மா அதிபர் (IGP) அலுவலகங்களை சிதைப்பது உட்பட அனைத்து சிறிய பாவங்களும் வருகின்றன.

முக்கிய பதவிகளுக்கான அரசியல் நியமனங்கள் காரணமாக தனது சொந்த அதிகாரிகளை தன்னால் கட்டுப்படுத்த முடியாது என ஐஜிபி பதிவு செய்துள்ளார். ‘சுயாதீனமான’ தேசிய பொலிஸ் ஆணைக்குழுவை ‘மீட்டமைப்பதாலோ’ அல்லது ஐஜிபியின் ‘சுயாதீனமான’ நியமனத்தினாலோ அந்த பரிதாப நிலையை வெறுமனே சரி செய்ய முடியாது. தேவாலயங்கள் மற்றும் ஹோட்டல்கள் மீதான 2019 ஜிஹாதித் தாக்குதல்கள் தொடர்பான கட்டளைப் பொறுப்பின் தோல்விக்காக பின்னர் சிறைக்குச் சென்ற ஒரு சிறந்த பொருத்தமற்ற ஐஜிபியின் 'யஹாபாலனயா' உதாரணத்தை நினைவுபடுத்துவதற்கு இந்த விஷயத்தை வாதிட விரும்புவோர் ஊக்குவிக்கப்படலாம். அரசியலமைப்பின் 19 வது திருத்தம் அது பிறந்த தருணத்தில் அழிவுக்கு உள்ளானது ஏன்.

சக்கரத்தை மீண்டும் கண்டுபிடிக்கும் மற்றொரு திருத்தத்தின் தலைவிதி இதுவாக இருக்கும் என்று சொல்லத் தேவையில்லை. 19வது திருத்தச் சட்டம் யாருடைய ஆட்சிக் காலத்தில் பிறப்பிக்கப்பட்டதோ, அதே பிரதமர், சிலரது கூச்சலுக்கு ஆளாகி அதே ஆசனத்திற்குத் திரும்பி, இன்னும் மோசமான காலங்கள் வரப்போவதாக எச்சரிக்கை விடுத்துள்ளார்.

அவரது எச்சரிக்கைகள் 'அரகயலா' இளைஞர்கள் தங்கள் ஐம்பது நாள் போராட்டத்தை குடியரசை விட அதிக வண்ணத்துடன் கொண்டாடினர், அரசியலமைப்பு திருத்தங்கள் சர்க்கஸில் எதிர்ப்பு தெரிவிக்கின்றன, மருத்துவமனைகளில் மருந்து பற்றாக்குறை உள்ளது என சில நிகழ்வுகள் தெரிவிக்கின்றன. நோயாளிகள் மருத்துவர்களை தாக்கியுள்ளனர்.
இலங்கைக்கு மீட்பர்கள் இல்லை’ என்பதை தெளிவாகச் சொல்ல வேண்டும்.

en.title: The Sri Lankan political establishment’s ‘dirty tricks’ department seems to be in full swing as the nation is presented with a dizzying array of constitutional amendments under various titles
body_lines: The Sri Lankan political establishment’s ‘dirty tricks’ department seems to be in full swing as the nation is presented with a dizzying array of constitutional amendments, variously titled ’21st Amendment’, ‘21A’, ’21B’, ‘22A’ and so forth.

It says much for the combined idiocy underlying this process of ‘constitutional reform,’ (for want of a better term), that even the originators of these amendments are uncertain as to which clause belongs to what. Their bewildered faces and incoherent explanations when microphones are brandished before them, illustrates this very well.

Other politicians sitting in the opposition benches meanwhile enter into violent quarrels over the correct numbering of the amendments. All in all, this is constitutional Babel, in the Biblical sense of that term.

That is, ‘a confusion of tongues’ with no one meaning what they say and very few knowing what they mean. Clearly, there are two strands of disparate opinion which have emerged among the ‘faithful.’ The first urges the conditional acceptance of the Ministry of Justice’s constitutional amendment, one hesitates to give this a number in good conscience.

This looks at resuscitating the yahapalanaya’ inspired 19th Amendment with some devious twists. It disregards even the minimum balance of the 19th Amendment with the President being enabled to hold ministerial portfolios.

This is even as President Gotabhaya Rajapaksa returns to public prominence as he recovers from the unsettling fury of the youth’s ‘Aragalaya’ (struggle) and consolidates himself. Increasing number of state institutions are brought under his control while he lectures secretaries of ministries on productively using state land to grow crops to prevent a food crisis.

This is rich in manifold respects. The very reason why Sri Lankans are facing the spectre of starvation is due to intemperate Presidential policy making, not only in forcing a ridiculous overnight ban on commercial fertiliser but also refusing to reverse that policy.

Desperate farmers begged for relief. Instead, they were told that the Army would be used to ‘compel’ them to farm. But let us put that aside for the time being. The Government’s constitutional reform proposals are being touted by a Minister of Justice, the same worthy who, during the ‘yahapalanaya’ period, boasted that he would never allow the incumbent President, (at the time, the former Defence Secretary), to be arrested. The magnanimity given to the President in the holding of ministerial portfolios has been strongly objected to by the Opposition.

We await with bated breath, the next act in this drama as well as the fate of the Bill proposed by the main Opposition Samagi Jana Balawegaya which went much farther in correcting the constitutional imbalance of power than the Government’s draft.

We may also beware of purported ‘concessions’ that never really concede power from the political establishment to the furious citizenry. Professional lobbies and advocacy groups have called for the composition of the Constitutional Council (CC) to be changed so that politicians or their nominees will not hold the balance of power. This was a major problem with the 19th Amendment. That is essential rectification, the 17th Amendment is a good example to follow.

But even granted all of this, there is deep and legitimate suspicion that these are patchwork attempts to deflect public anger, to turn the focus towards constitutional reform which has never actually impacted on the populace.

And so we come to the second strand of opinion which ridicules the very idea of a President and a Parliament that allowed the nation to come to bankruptcy, its citizens to be humiliated and beggared before the world, being tasked with constitutional reform. That ridicule emphasizes that  this undermines the ‘Aragalaya’s call for ‘system reforms’ and for ‘Gota to Go.’

So, whence the Republic as its Fiftieth Anniversary arrives and departs without anyone the wiser? To answer that question, it must be recognised that the ‘Republic’ has never been a ‘Republic’ in the true meaning of that term. This is not only in the Rajapaksa age as some would like to believe.

Before the advent of the Rajapaksas, the ‘denial’ of the Republic was just better papered over by the gloss of ‘apparent’ conformity to the Rule of Law even when the same constitutional games were being played. The difference was that those games were far more sophisticated than the rough and tumble with crooks, charlatans and conners now.

I say this with force for there is a larger thrust to this argument. Even when the best judgments were being handed down by the Supreme Court (think, the mid to late eighties) upholding constitutional rights, from the right not to be arbitrarily arrested to freedoms of speech and expression, those were victories limited to constitutional theory scarcely percolating to the street. As public interest lawyers then, (not to be confused with the ugly commercialisation of public interest litigation later), it was thrilling to see constitutional rights advanced.

Even so, much depended on which President was in power at the time, even the (so-called) best legal brains thought strategy in that light. Little wonder then, that the ‘independence of the judiciary’ received short shrift as the apex court of the land and the Office of the Chief Justice became the target of unprecedented controversy during the Kumaratunga Presidency and after. So when the state of the Republic is reflected upon, awkward questions must be asked. How and why not only the Bar but also legal academia (largely) silent when the Bench embroiled itself in the thickets of political controversy?

Or when contempt of court was openly threatened against dissenters and when the judicial institution was taken in a determinedly political direction, was a Nelsonian eye turned? Those dagger thrusts were aimed at independent institutions while ‘many slept.’

In later decades, the very many ‘impeachments’ of Chief Justices and the loss of lustre of the judicial institution were all illustrative thereof. From that great sin comes all lesser sins including the crucifixion of Sri Lankan constitutionalism on the altar of political expediency, the monstrous growth of the Executive Presidency, the warping of the Offices of the Attorney General and the Inspector-General of Police (IGP).

The IGP has gone on record stating that he cannot control his own officers due to political appointments to key posts. That pathetic state cannot be simply rectified by ‘restoring’ an ‘independent’ National Police Commission or the ‘independent’ appointment of the IGP. Those inclined to argue the point may be encouraged to recall the ‘yahapalanaya’ example of an eminently unsuitable IGP who later went to jail over failures of command responsibility regarding the 2019 jihadist attacks on churches and hotels. All this is precisely why the 19th Amendment to the Constitution was doomed at the very point that it was born.

Needless to say, this will be the fate of yet another amendment which reinvents the wheel. The same Prime Minister in whose term, the 19th Amendment was birthed, has returned to that same seat to the hurrahs of some, issuing warnings of ‘worse times’ yet to come.

His warnings have little impact on the youth of the ‘Aragayala’ who celebrated their fifty days of struggle with a tad more colour than the Republic, protesting at a circus of constitutional amendments as hospitals run short of medicine with some instances reported where relatives of critically ill patients have assaulted doctors.
There are no saviours’ for Sri Lanka, let it be said plainly.
